concepts.title: Whole Numbers and Integers
concepts.definition: Whole numbers are {0, 1, 2, 3, ...}. Integers extend whole numbers to include negative numbers, i.e., {..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ...}.
examples: −5, 0 and 12 are integers; 2.5 is not an integer.
Whole numbers are a subset of integers.

concepts.title: Representation on Number Line
concepts.definition: Integers can be shown on a horizontal line with zero at the centre, positive numbers to the right and negative numbers to the left, equally spaced.
examples: On the number line −3 is to the left of 2, so −3 < 2.

concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction of Integers
concepts.definition: Adding and subtracting integers follows rules based on their signs. Same signs add magnitudes and keep the sign; different signs subtract magnitudes and take the sign of the larger magnitude.
examples: 5 + (−3) = 2, because 5 − 3 = 2 and 5 has larger magnitude.

concepts.title: Multiplication of Integers
concepts.definition: Multiplication of integers follows sign rules: product of two integers with same sign is positive, with different signs is negative.

concepts.title: Division of Integers
concepts.definition: Division of integers uses the same sign rules as multiplication: quotient of two integers with same sign is positive, with different signs is negative.

concepts.title: Properties of Operations on Integers
concepts.definition: Integers are closed under addition, subtraction and multiplication and satisfy commutative, associative and distributive properties for these operations.
formulae: a + b = b + a (commutative for addition)
a × b = b × a (commutative for multiplication)
(a + b) + c = a + (b + c) (associative for addition)
(a × b) × c = a × (b × c) (associative for multiplication)
a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c (distributive)

concepts.title: Types of Fractions
concepts.definition: Fractions can be proper (numerator < denominator), improper (numerator ≥ denominator) or mixed (a whole number plus a proper fraction).
examples: 3/4 is a proper fraction, 7/3 is improper, 2 1/5 is a mixed fraction.

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions and Simplification
concepts.definition: Equivalent fractions represent the same value; they can be obtained by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same non‑zero number.
examples: 6/8 simplified by dividing numerator and denominator by 2 gives 3/4.

concepts.title: Operations on Fractions
concepts.definition: Fractions are added or subtracted using a common denominator; multiplication multiplies numerators and denominators; division multiplies by the reciprocal.

concepts.title: Decimals and Place Value
concepts.definition: Decimals have a whole number part and a fractional part separated by a decimal point, with place values tenths, hundredths, thousandths and so on.
examples: In 4.327, 3 is tenths, 2 is hundredths and 7 is thousandths.

concepts.title: Operations on Decimals
concepts.definition: Addition and subtraction of decimals align the decimal points; multiplication and division follow whole‑number rules while managing decimal places.

concepts.title: Conversion Between Fractions and Decimals
concepts.definition: Fractions with denominators like 10, 100, 1000 can be written as decimals, and terminating decimals can be written as fractions.

concepts.title: Data and Frequency
concepts.definition: Data are collected facts or numbers; frequency of a value is the number of times it occurs in the data.
examples: If the marks 5, 7, 5, 8, 5 occur, the frequency of 5 is 3.

concepts.title: Tally Marks and Tables
concepts.definition: Tally marks are quick strokes used to count frequencies; grouped into fives for easy reading and organised in tables.
examples: |||| represents 4 and ||||/ represents 5 in tally marks.

concepts.title: Bar Graphs
concepts.definition: Bar graphs represent data using rectangular bars with heights proportional to frequencies on a set of axes.
examples: A bar graph can show the number of students choosing different sports.

concepts.title: Mean, Median and Mode
concepts.definition: Mean is the average of data, median is the middle value when data are arranged in order, and mode is the most frequent value.
formulae: Mean = (sum of all observations) / (number of observations)
examples: For data 2, 4, 6: mean = (2 + 4 + 6) / 3 = 4; median = 4; any value occurring most often is the mode.

concepts.title: Chance and Probability (Informal)
concepts.definition: Chance describes how likely an event is to happen, using terms like certain, likely, unlikely and impossible.
examples: Getting a number greater than 6 on a standard die is impossible.
Getting a head when tossing a coin is equally likely as getting a tail.

concepts.title: Variables and Constants
concepts.definition: A variable is a symbol (like x, y) that can take different values; a constant is a fixed number.
examples: In 3x + 5, x is a variable and 3, 5 are constants (3 also a coefficient).

concepts.title: Algebraic Expressions
concepts.definition: An algebraic expression is formed by combining variables and constants using operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
examples: 4x + 7, 3y − 2 and 2a + 3b + 5 are algebraic expressions.

concepts.title: Equations and Solutions
concepts.definition: An equation states that two expressions are equal and usually contains a variable; the solution is the value of the variable that makes the equation true.
examples: x + 5 = 9 is an equation; x = 4 is its solution.

concepts.title: Solving One‑Step Linear Equations
concepts.definition: Simple equations of type ax + b = c are solved by doing the same operation on both sides to isolate the variable.

concepts.title: Applications of Simple Equations
concepts.definition: Word problems can be translated into equations in one variable and solved using the same methods.
examples: If a number increased by 9 gives 20, let the number be x: x + 9 = 20 ⇒ x = 11.

concepts.title: Basic Terms: Point, Line, Ray, Line Segment
concepts.definition: A point shows a position; a line extends endlessly in both directions; a ray has one endpoint and extends endlessly in one direction; a line segment has two endpoints.
examples: A pencil tip represents a point, an edge of a ruler can model a line segment.

concepts.title: Types of Angles
concepts.definition: Angles are classified as acute (0°–90°), right (90°), obtuse (90°–180°), straight (180°), reflex (180°–360°) and complete (360°).
examples: A corner of a square is a right angle; a slice less than a quarter circle is an acute angle.

concepts.title: Pairs of Angles
concepts.definition: Adjacent, linear pair, vertically opposite, complementary and supplementary angles describe relationships between two angles.
formulae: Angles in a linear pair sum to 180°
examples: If two angles form a straight line, they are supplementary.
Opposite angles where two lines intersect are vertically opposite and equal.

concepts.title: Intersecting and Parallel Lines
concepts.definition: Intersecting lines meet at a point; parallel lines never meet, however far extended.
examples: Railway tracks are approximately parallel lines.

concepts.title: Classification of Triangles
concepts.definition: Triangles can be classified by sides as scalene, isosceles or equilateral and by angles as acute, right or obtuse.
examples: An equilateral triangle has all sides equal and all angles 60°.
A right triangle has one 90° angle.

concepts.title: Angle Sum Property
concepts.definition: The sum of the three interior angles of a triangle is always 180 degrees.
examples: If two angles are 50° and 60°, the third is 180° − (50° + 60°) = 70°.

concepts.title: Exterior Angle Property
concepts.definition: An exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite angles.
formulae: Exterior angle = sum of two interior opposite angles
examples: If an exterior angle is 120° and one opposite interior angle is 50°, the other is 70°.

concepts.title: Triangle Inequality
concepts.definition: In a triangle, the sum of any two sides is greater than the third side.
examples: Sides 2 cm, 3 cm, 6 cm cannot form a triangle because 2 + 3 is not greater than 6.

concepts.title: Medians and Altitudes
concepts.definition: A median joins a vertex of a triangle to the midpoint of the opposite side; an altitude is a perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side or its extension.
examples: Every triangle has three medians and three altitudes.

concepts.title: Congruent Figures
concepts.definition: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size; they can be made to coincide by shifting, rotating or flipping.
examples: Two coins of the same denomination are congruent circles.

concepts.title: Congruence of Triangles
concepts.definition: Two triangles are congruent if all their corresponding sides and angles are equal in measure.
examples: If △ABC is placed exactly over △PQR and all vertices match, they are congruent.

concepts.title: Congruence Criteria: SSS, SAS, ASA, RHS
concepts.definition: Triangles are congruent if they satisfy any of these conditions: all three corresponding sides equal (SSS), two sides and included angle equal (SAS), two angles and included side equal (ASA) or right angle, hypotenuse and one side equal (RHS).
examples: If three sides of one triangle equal three sides of another, they are congruent by SSS.
Two right triangles with equal hypotenuse and one corresponding side are congruent by RHS.

concepts.title: Ratio and Proportion
concepts.definition: Ratio compares two quantities of the same kind; proportion states that two ratios are equal.
formulae: a : b = c : d ⇔ a/b = c/d
examples: The ratio of 2 to 5 is 2 : 5.

concepts.title: Percentage
concepts.definition: Percentage is a way of expressing a number as a fraction of 100.
examples: If a student scores 45 out of 50, percentage = (45/50) × 100 = 90%.

concepts.title: Profit and Loss
concepts.definition: Profit is the gain when selling price exceeds cost price; loss is the amount by which cost price exceeds selling price.
examples: If CP is ₹100 and SP is ₹120, profit = ₹20 and profit% = 20%.

concepts.title: Simple Interest
concepts.definition: Simple interest is interest calculated on the original principal only for the entire period.
examples: On ₹1000 at 5% per year for 2 years, SI = (1000 × 5 × 2) / 100 = ₹100.

concepts.title: Definition of Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: A rational number is any number that can be written in the form p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0.
examples: 2/3, −5/7 and 4 (as 4/1) are rational numbers.

concepts.title: Equivalent Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: Rational numbers that represent the same point on the number line are equivalent; they can be obtained by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same non‑zero integer.
examples: 1/2, 2/4 and −3/−6 are equivalent rational numbers.

concepts.title: Standard Form of Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: A rational number is in standard form when it is written with a positive denominator and numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1.
examples: −6/8 simplified to standard form is −3/4.

concepts.title: Operations on Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: Addition, subtraction, multiplication and division of rational numbers follow the same rules as for fractions, with sign rules applied.

concepts.title: Constructing a Line Parallel to a Given Line
concepts.definition: Using a ruler and compass or set‑square, a line can be drawn through a given point parallel to a given line.
examples: Through point P outside line l, draw line m parallel to l using a set‑square.

concepts.title: Constructing Triangles: SSS
concepts.definition: A triangle can be constructed when the lengths of all three sides are known, using a ruler and compass.
examples: Construct △ABC given AB, BC and CA by first drawing one side and then using two circles with given radii to locate the third vertex.

concepts.title: Constructing Triangles: SAS and ASA
concepts.definition: Triangles can be constructed when two sides and the included angle (SAS) or two angles and the included side (ASA) are known.
examples: To construct a triangle with sides AB and AC and included angle at A, draw AB, construct the angle at A and mark AC.

concepts.title: Constructing Right Triangles (RHS)
concepts.definition: Right‑angled triangles can be constructed when hypotenuse and one side are given, using a right angle at the appropriate vertex.
examples: Construct a right triangle with hypotenuse 5 cm and one side 3 cm using a right‑angle tool and compass.

concepts.title: Perimeter
concepts.definition: Perimeter is the total length of the boundary of a closed figure.
examples: A rectangle 5 cm by 3 cm has perimeter 2 × (5 + 3) = 16 cm.

concepts.title: Area of Rectangle and Square
concepts.definition: Area is the region enclosed by a closed figure. For rectangles and squares it equals the product of side lengths.
examples: A square of side 4 cm has area 16 cm².

concepts.title: Area of Triangle and Parallelogram (Introductory)
concepts.definition: Area of a triangle is half the product of its base and height; area of a parallelogram is product of base and corresponding height.
examples: A triangle with base 6 cm and height 4 cm has area (1/2) × 6 × 4 = 12 cm².

concepts.title: Terms, Coefficients and Factors
concepts.definition: An algebraic expression is made of terms; each term has a numeric coefficient and one or more variable factors.
examples: In 5xy, 5 is the coefficient and x, y are variable factors.
In 7a − 3, 7a and −3 are terms.

concepts.title: Like and Unlike Terms
concepts.definition: Like terms have exactly the same variable part (including powers); unlike terms differ in variable part or exponents.
examples: 3x and −5x are like terms; 2x and 2y are unlike terms.
4a²b and −7a²b are like terms.

concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction of Expressions
concepts.definition: Algebraic expressions are added or subtracted by combining like terms.

concepts.title: Multiplication of a Monomial by a Monomial
concepts.definition: Multiplying two monomials multiplies their coefficients and adds exponents of like variables.

concepts.title: Multiplication of a Monomial by a Polynomial
concepts.definition: To multiply a monomial by a polynomial, multiply the monomial with each term of the polynomial and add results.

concepts.title: Exponents and Expanded Form
concepts.definition: Exponents are a shorthand notation for repeated multiplication of the same number.

concepts.title: Laws of Exponents (Same Base)
concepts.definition: Exponents with the same base follow specific rules for multiplication and division.

concepts.title: Power of a Power and Product
concepts.definition: Raising a power to another power multiplies exponents; raising a product to a power raises each factor.

concepts.title: Negative and Zero Exponents (Introductory)
concepts.definition: Negative exponents represent reciprocals and zero exponent represents 1 for any non‑zero base.

concepts.title: Standard Form (Scientific Notation)
concepts.definition: Very large or very small numbers can be written in standard form as a × 10^n, where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer.

concepts.title: Line Symmetry
concepts.definition: A figure has line symmetry if it can be folded along a line so that both halves coincide exactly.
examples: An equilateral triangle has 3 lines of symmetry; a square has 4.

concepts.title: Lines of Symmetry in Standard Figures
concepts.definition: Regular figures often have multiple lines of symmetry depending on the number of equal sides and angles.
examples: A regular pentagon has 5 lines of symmetry.
A circle has infinitely many lines of symmetry.

concepts.title: Relation with Reflections
concepts.definition: Reflection in a mirror produces a symmetric image across the mirror line; the mirror line is a line of symmetry.
examples: Letters like A, H and O show line symmetry when reflected across certain lines.

concepts.title: 2D and 3D Shapes
concepts.definition: 2D shapes have length and breadth (like squares, circles); 3D shapes have length, breadth and height (like cubes, spheres).
examples: A rectangle is 2D; a cuboid like a brick is 3D.

concepts.title: Faces, Edges and Vertices
concepts.definition: Faces are flat surfaces of a solid, edges are line segments where faces meet, and vertices are points where edges meet.
examples: A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.

concepts.title: Nets of 3D Shapes
concepts.definition: A net is a 2D pattern that can be folded to form a 3D solid.
examples: A cross‑shaped arrangement of 6 equal squares can be folded into a cube.

concepts.title: Different Views of 3D Objects
concepts.definition: A 3D object can look different from front, side and top; these projections help visualise and draw solids.
examples: A cylinder looks like a rectangle from the side and a circle from the top.
